description: The provided image consists solely of a generic software notification icon bearing the text 'Check for updates' on a gray rounded-square background, and it contains no discernible materials science figure, micrograph, spectrum, plot, or schematic. No sub-panels, coordinate axes, scale bars, sample identifiers, phase labels, or quantitative data are visible or readable from the image pixels. Consequently, it is impossible to visually identify whether this figure corresponds to engineering stress-strain curves, SEM fracture surfaces, TEM precipitate images, EBSD orientation maps, XRD patterns, or APT reconstructions discussed in the accompanying text extraction summary. The paper context reports a systematic investigation of 0Al and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels, detailing dual precipitation of nanoscale NiAl clusters and ε-carbides, martensitic multilevel structures, and enhanced toughness (~1.9 GPa tensile strength, KIC 108.0 MPa·m^(1/2) for 1.3Al-2h). However, because the actual scientific image is absent or obstructed by this UI element, none of these details can be confirmed as visual content of this figure, and no figure-specific scientific conclusions can be drawn from the image itself.

Figure 1 presents the mechanical properties of two low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels, 0Al and 1.3Al (containing ~1.3 wt% Al), in the as-water-quenched (WQ) condition and after tempering at 180 °C for 2 h. Subfigure (a) displays the engineering stress–strain curves, showing that both steels possess high ultimate tensile strength (UTS) in the as-quenched state—reaching approximately 2294 MPa for 0Al and 2145 MPa for 1.3Al—but with limited ductility, especially for 1.3Al which fractured before reaching its maximum load. After tempering, the UTS of both steels decreases to below 2000 MPa (1925 MPa for 0Al and 1876 MPa for 1.3Al), while the total elongation significantly improves to about 13.5–13.7 %. Subfigure (b) summarizes the Charpy impact-absorbing energy (KU₂), revealing a dramatic toughness enhancement upon tempering: the 1.3Al steel rises from 23 ± 2 J to 80 ± 3 J (~3.5× increase), surpassing the tempered 0Al steel (65 ± 3 J). These results demonstrate that the 180 °C tempering treatment achieves an exceptional combination of ~1.9 GPa tensile strength and markedly improved toughness in the 1.3Al steel, supporting the central thesis that high-density coherent NiAl clusters provide effective toughening without severely compromising strength.

Figure 2 comprises two panels characterizing the microstructural basis of the 0Al and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels in the tempered condition. Panel (a) is a scanning electron micrograph of the 1.3Al steel displaying a tempered martensitic lath structure, with a transmission electron microscopy inset (scale bar 0.2 μm) revealing finer lamellar features within the laths. Panel (b) presents X-ray diffraction patterns of both the 0Al and 1.3Al steels, indexed to martensite (α) and austenite (γ) peaks including (111)γ, (110)α, (200)γ, (200)α, (211)α, and (220)α. The patterns are annotated with calculated dislocation densities of approximately 2.01×10^15 m^-2 for the 0Al steel and 2.20×10^15 m^-2 for the 1.3Al steel, indicating a slight increase upon Al addition. Citation text reports retained austenite contents of 1.0% for 0Al and 1.8% for 1.3Al derived from these patterns. Together, the figure establishes that both steels possess tempered martensitic matrices with marginally different dislocation densities and retained austenite fractions, providing the structural baseline for evaluating the role of high-density NiAl clusters in toughening.

Figure 3 presents a comprehensive TEM investigation comparing the tempered microstructures of 0Al and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels after tempering at 180 °C for 2 h. Panels (a) and (d) show bright-field images of lath martensite, where the 0Al steel exhibits sharp lath boundaries while the 1.3Al steel displays a high density of dislocations within the laths. Panels (b) and (e) use diffraction contrast to reveal thin-film retained austenite at lath boundaries in both steels, accompanied by selected-area electron diffraction patterns confirming a Kurdjumov–Sachs orientation relationship between austenite and martensite. Panels (c) and (f) highlight needle-like ε-carbides within the martensitic matrix, with corresponding diffraction patterns indexing the hexagonal close-packed structure of ε-carbide. Together, these images establish that both steels contain tempered martensite, boundary-retained austenite, and intralath ε-carbides, while the 1.3Al steel exhibits higher dislocation density, providing structural evidence for its enhanced strength and toughness.

This figure presents a hierarchical multiscale comparison of multilevel martensitic structures in two experimental low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels, labeled 0Al and 1.3Al, as shown by the visible row markers. Panel (a) displays EBSD inverse-pole-figure maps revealing prior austenite grains with a 20 μm scale bar; panel (b) shows SEM micrographs of martensitic packets at 10 μm scale with yellow brackets denoting packet size; panel (c) provides EBSD orientation maps of martensitic blocks at 5 μm scale indicating block width; and panel (d) offers TEM bright-field images of martensitic laths at 0.2 μm scale marking lath width. Visually, both steels exhibit closely comparable structural dimensions across all four levels. The accompanying text reports average prior austenite grain sizes of 26.1 ± 9.4 μm for 0Al and 23.4 ± 5.9 μm for 1.3Al, with similar packet sizes (~3.4–3.5 μm), block widths (~0.8–0.9 μm), and lath widths (~0.23–0.24 μm). These minimal differences support the central conclusion that multilevel martensitic unit size is not the dominant factor governing impact toughness (KU2) and fracture toughness (KIC), and that differences in precipitation behavior instead govern the toughening response.

Multiscale characterization of nanoprecipitates in tempered 1.3Al ultrahigh-strength steel. (a) APT 5 at.% C isoconcentration surface revealing needle-like ε-carbides uniformly dispersed in the matrix. (b) HRTEM image and corresponding FFT (inset) identifying the precipitate as hexagonal ε-carbide viewed along the [110] zone axis within the bcc martensitic matrix. (c) Proximity histogram showing sharp carbon enrichment in ε-carbides with slight Ni and Al segregation at their cores. (d) APT 10 at.% Ni+Al isoconcentration surface revealing near-spherical NiAl clusters homogeneously distributed. (e) HRTEM image and FFT (inset) confirming the NiAl clusters possess a B2-ordered bcc structure along the [001] zone axis and are fully coherent with the matrix. (f) Proximity histogram demonstrating Ni and Al enrichment in the clusters to approximately 13 at.%—below the stoichiometric NiAl ratio—indicating an early-stage nucleation state, accompanied by minor co-segregation of Si, Cr and Mo. Together, the figure establishes that ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate independently during tempering without elemental overlap, providing direct structural and compositional evidence for the dual-precipitation toughening mechanism.

Fig. 6 presents APT characterization of the 1.3Al steel, revealing three-dimensional atomic-scale distributions and compositional partitioning among ε-carbides, NiAl clusters, and segregated solutes. Panel A shows the atomic map of Ni (green), Al (cyan), and C (brown) across an ε-carbide cross-section; the accompanying proximity histogram traces compositional variations from the matrix into dispersed NiAl clusters, showing pronounced Ni and Al enrichment with low carbon in the surrounding matrix. The central reconstruction (B) visualizes 10 at.% NiAl (green) and 5 at.% C (brown) isoconcentration surfaces, illustrating high-density nanoscale NiAl clusters coexisting with elongated ε-carbides (scale bar 20 nm). Panel C displays 4 at.% Cr+Mo (red) and 7 at.% Si (purple) isoconcentration surfaces and a proximity histogram of segregated NiAl clusters, revealing marked Si, Cr, and Mo enrichment in banded regions; the histogram indicates Si rises to approximately 11 at.%, Cr to about 6 at.%, and Mo to roughly 1–2 at.%. These observations support that ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate and grow independently during tempering without competitive elemental partitioning between C and Ni/Al, and that Si-enriched intragranular defects provide heterogeneous nucleation sites for NiAl clusters.

Figure 7 presents Charpy impact load-deflection and load-energy-displacement curves for tempered low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels. Panel (a) compares the 0Al baseline steel and the 1.3Al steel containing high-density NiAl clusters. The 0Al steel reaches a maximum load (P_M) of 39.7 kN at a deflection of 2.06 mm, whereas the 1.3Al steel peaks at 38.6 kN with a larger deflection of 2.55 mm, indicating greater plastic deformation capacity prior to fracture. Panels (b) and (c) partition the impact energy for each steel. For the 0Al steel, the crack initiation energy (E_i) is 45.0 J, the crack propagation energy (E_p) is 17.4 J, and the total Charpy impact energy (KU_2) is 62.4 J. For the 1.3Al steel, these values increase to 62.0 J, 21.0 J, and 83.0 J, respectively, representing 37.8% higher E_i and 20.7% higher E_p. These data demonstrate that introducing coherent NiAl clusters substantially improves toughness by enhancing resistance to both crack initiation and propagation, consistent with homogenized dislocation distribution and progressive strain delocalization.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of impact fracture morphologies for 0Al and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels. Subfigures (a) and (d) show macroscopic fracture surfaces at 1 mm scale for the 0Al and 1.3Al steels, respectively. Subfigures (b) and (c) display higher-magnification microscopic features at 100 μm and 25 μm scales of the 0Al steel, where yellow dashed circles highlight primary dimples and blue dashed circles in (c) mark quasi-cleavage regions; green arrows indicate cracks or tearing directions. In contrast, subfigures (e) and (f) reveal microscopic fracture surfaces of the 1.3Al steel at matching magnifications, with yellow dashed circles in (e) indicating growing dimples and green arrows pointing to tearing ridges. Compared to the 0Al steel, the 1.3Al steel exhibits a notably higher density of larger and deeper dimples, more prominent tearing ridges, and greater overall surface roughness. These fractographic features support that the 1.3Al steel undergoes enhanced ductile fracture through void nucleation, growth, and coalescence, dissipating substantial impact energy and contributing to its superior toughness relative to the 0Al counterpart.

Figure 9 presents cross-sectional EBSD characterization near the main cracks of impact-fractured 0Al and 1.3Al low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels. Panels (a) and (c) display BC+IPF+GB maps showing grain orientations and crack-tip microstructures for the 0Al and 1.3Al steels, respectively, with yellow-boxed insets highlighting magnified regions. Panels (b) and (d) show corresponding KAM maps for local strain distribution. The 0Al steel exhibits an average KAM value of 1.16° and shows localized red/yellow high-strain regions adjacent to the main crack alongside blue undeformed matrix areas, indicating inhomogeneous strain partitioning. The 1.3Al steel displays an average KAM of 1.31° near the fracture surface with a more uniform green-dominated distribution across both the fracture surface and surrounding matrix. The comparison indicates that the 1.3Al steel achieves more homogeneous strain distribution near the crack, and the accompanying text reports that this uniformity arises from NiAl-cluster-promoted dislocation homogenization, which facilitates stress transfer and coordinated deformation to enhance fracture toughness.

Figure 10 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fracture vertical-section microstructures from fracture toughness specimens of low alloy ultrahigh strength steels. Panels (a) and (b) are low-magnification images with a 50 μm scale bar for the 0Al and 1.3Al steel specimens, respectively, where cyan arrows trace the main crack paths. The 0Al steel in (a) exhibits a relatively straight main crack with minimal deflection and sparse micro-voids. In contrast, the 1.3Al steel in (b) shows frequent crack deflections and a higher density of micro-voids along the crack path. Panels (c) and (d) are magnified images with a 10 μm scale bar corresponding to the blue boxed regions in (a) and (b), respectively. Panel (c) reveals crack tip passivation in the 0Al steel, while panel (d) for the 1.3Al steel displays secondary cracks and micro-voids (approximately 5–10 μm) located beneath the main crack, with fine cracks interconnecting these voids indicating coalescence. The comparison demonstrates that the 1.3Al steel undergoes extensive crack deflection, secondary cracking, and void coalescence, which increase crack propagation distance and promote energy dissipation. These microstructural observations support the core conclusion that high-density NiAl clusters enhance toughness by inducing crack tortuosity and blunting mechanisms, consistent with the reported higher crack propagation energy and fracture toughness in the 1.3Al steel relative to the 0Al counterpart.
